quiz_questions.question_text: What determines pitch?
quiz_questions.explanation: The fundamental frequency determines pitch; higher harmonics determine timbre. Key Concepts — Pitch & Frequency Pitch Pitch is the perceived frequency of a sound — higher frequency = higher pitch. The other options — 'Amplitude', 'Wavelength', and 'Speed' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What frequency is concert A (A4)?
quiz_questions.explanation: A4 = 440 Hz → A5 = 880 Hz. The other options — '262 Hz', '880 Hz', and '1000 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an octave?
quiz_questions.explanation: Frequency and Musical Scales An octave = doubling of frequency. The other options — 'A halving of frequency', 'An increase of 100 Hz', and 'A change in amplitude' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is timbre?

options.text: The tone quality distinguishing different instruments

quiz_questions.explanation: Timbre (Tone Quality) Two instruments playing the same note sound different because of their harmonic content (overtones). The other options — 'The volume of a sound', 'The pitch of a note', and 'The speed of sound' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines timbre?

options.text: The mix of harmonics in the sound

quiz_questions.explanation: The fundamental frequency determines pitch; higher harmonics determine timbre. Timbre (Tone Quality) Two instruments playing the same note sound different because of their harmonic content (overtones). The other options — 'Only the fundamental frequency', 'Only the amplitude', and 'The speed of the wave' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: How does the ear perceive frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: The human ear perceives frequency logarithmically — equal ratios sound like equal intervals. The other options — 'Linearly', 'Exponentially', and 'It doesn't perceive frequency' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: A4 is 440 Hz. What is A5?
quiz_questions.explanation: A4 = 440 Hz → A5 = 880 Hz. The other options — '440 Hz', '220 Hz', and '660 Hz' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What determines the pitch of a musical sound?

options.text: Frequency — higher frequency produces higher perceived pitch

quiz_questions.explanation: Pitch is the perceptual correlate of frequency. Higher f → higher pitch.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is timbre (tone quality)?

options.text: Quality of sound determined by the harmonic content (which overtones are present and their amplitudes)
How high in pitch a sound is

quiz_questions.explanation: Same pitch and loudness from violin vs. flute: different harmonic spectra → different timbre.

quiz_questions.question_text: Middle A (A4) standard tuning frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: A4=440 Hz: international tuning standard for musical pitch since 1939.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes pitch?

options.text: Perceptual quality corresponding primarily to frequency; higher f → higher pitch; octave=doubling of frequency

quiz_questions.explanation: Pitch is subjective. Frequency is objective. Closely related but not identical.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an octave?

options.text: Interval between two notes with a frequency ratio of 2:1; doubling frequency raises pitch by one octave

quiz_questions.explanation: A4=440 Hz, A5=880 Hz, A3=220 Hz. Western music divides octave into 12 equal semitones.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a harmonic series?

options.text: Integer multiples of fundamental: f₁, 2f₁, 3f₁...; relative amplitudes determine timbre of an instrument

quiz_questions.explanation: Fourier theorem: any periodic sound = fundamental + harmonics with specific amplitudes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes fundamental frequency?

options.text: Lowest natural frequency (first harmonic) that an instrument or resonating body can produce
Any one of the resonant frequencies

quiz_questions.explanation: f₁ determines perceived pitch. Overtones (harmonics 2,3,...) enrich the timbre.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes frequency resolution of the human ear?

options.text: Ear can distinguish two frequencies differing by about 0.3% at midrange (roughly 3 Hz at 1000 Hz)
Cannot distinguish close frequencies at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Basilar membrane maps frequency to position. Hair cells tuned to different frequencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes critical band?

options.text: Frequency band within which sounds interact in the cochlea; intervals narrower than critical band sound dissonant
Frequency at which hearing is most sensitive

quiz_questions.explanation: Critical bands explain musical consonance/dissonance. Narrower than critical band: harsh combination.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the mel scale?

options.text: Perceptual pitch scale where equal intervals correspond to equally perceived pitch differences; non-linear above ~1000 Hz

quiz_questions.explanation: Mel scale: psychoacoustic. 1000 Hz = 1000 mel. Used in speech processing.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes vocal pitch production?

options.text: Vocal cords vibrate at fundamental frequency determined by tension and mass; formants shape the timbre
Lungs vibrate directly to produce sound

quiz_questions.explanation: Vocal cord tension sets fundamental pitch. Vocal tract formants create vowel quality and timbre.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes absolute pitch (perfect pitch)?

options.text: Rare ability to identify or reproduce a musical pitch without an external reference
Ability to hear up to 20 kHz

quiz_questions.explanation: About 1 in 10,000 people have absolute pitch. Most musicians rely on relative pitch.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes pitch bend?

options.text: Smooth continuous change in frequency (pitch) during a note, used for expressiveness in music
Change in volume during a note
Change in timbre during a note

quiz_questions.explanation: Pitch bend: gradual frequency change. Vibrato: periodic frequency modulation at ~5-7 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: String vibrating at 220 Hz. Frequency one octave higher?
quiz_questions.explanation: Octave up = double frequency. 220 × 2 = 440 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: A4 = 440 Hz. What is A5?
quiz_questions.explanation: One octave up = double frequency. 440 × 2 = 880 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: Piano string length halves (tension unchanged). New frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: For string: f = v/(2L). Half L → double f (one octave higher).

quiz_questions.question_text: Open string at 100 Hz. First overtone (2nd harmonic) frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: First overtone = 2nd harmonic = 2f₁ = 200 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tuner shows A at 438 Hz (should be 440 Hz). The pitch is:
quiz_questions.explanation: 438 < 440 Hz → slightly flat. Tighten string to raise pitch.

quiz_questions.question_text: 440 Hz and 443 Hz played together. Beat frequency?
quiz_questions.explanation: |443 − 440| = 3 Hz. Audible as 3 pulsations per second.

quiz_questions.question_text: Pipe resonates at 100 Hz, 200 Hz, 300 Hz. Pipe type?

options.text: Open-open — supports all harmonics with f₁=100 Hz

quiz_questions.explanation: Open-open: f₁, 2f₁, 3f₁... = 100, 200, 300 Hz. All harmonics present.

quiz_questions.question_text: Normal male speaking voice fundamental frequency range?
quiz_questions.explanation: Male voice: ~80–180 Hz fundamental. Female: ~165–255 Hz.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why do larger animals generally have lower-pitched calls?

options.text: Larger vocal structures → lower fundamental frequency (f ∝ 1/√m and 1/L)

quiz_questions.explanation: f ∝ 1/L (length) and 1/√m (mass). Elephant: low rumbles. Hummingbird: high pitched.

quiz_questions.question_text: 440 Hz and 448 Hz tones interfere. Beat period (time between beats)?
